title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Fibrillation is a type of arrhythmia where the heart beats irregularly and may be too

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: For certain types of arrhythmias, a catheter ablation procedure may be necessary to stop the heart tissue from causing the arrhythmia.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: After numbing a small area in the groin with a needle, the doctor will insert a short hollow tube called a catheter sheath into the femoral vein.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Next, a long flexible tube called a catheter will be inserted through the sheath.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: The doctor will guide the catheter to the heart through a blood vessel that goes to the heart, called the inferior vena-cava. The location and progress of the catheter will be monitored.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: When the catheter reaches the heart, the doctor will guide it to the area that is causing the arrhythmia.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: The doctor will find the problem area is using a 3D map of the electrical activity of the patient's heart.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: The tip of the catheter will emit either hot energy or cold energy to ablate the tissue in this area. The patient makes the treated area stop working.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: For an atrial arrhythmia, a doctor will ablate the atrial tissue causing it. If the affected tissues are small, well-defined areas, the procedure is called focal ablation.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Or if the affected tissues are larger areas with more complex rhythm disturbances, the doctor may perform a procedure called ablation remodeling.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: Both types of ablation restore normal electrical impulses and prevent an arrhythmia from happening.

title: ANH14137 Catheter Ablation
text_en: If the cause of the arrhythmia is in the ventricle, the doctor can do either focal ablation or ablation remodeling to treat more complex arrhythmias of the ventricle.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The heart has four muscular pumping chambers, the right atrium, the left atrium, the right ventricle, and the left ventricle.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The left ventricle pumps blood out through a large artery called the aorta to supply the body with the oxygen and nutrients it needs.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: When left-sided heart failure, the left ventricle is not able to pump enough blood to the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The two types of left-handed heart failure are diastolic, where the left ventricle can't relax enough to fill with blood, and more commonly systolic, where the left ventricle can't contract strongly enough to push blood to the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: In severe or end-stage heart failure, the heart is getting weaker and doesn't respond to medication.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: For left-sided end-stage systolic heart failure, a doctor may recommend an implantable left ventricular assist device, or LVAD, to help the heart pump enough blood to the body. An LVAD is a small mechanical pump implanted inside the chest or abdomen that takes over the job of the weak left ventricle.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: Blood flows from the left ventricle through an inflow tube to the pump. The pump pushes the blood through an outflow tube into the aorta. From here, the blood travels normally from the aorta to the rest of the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: A cable, called a drive line, connects the LVAD pump inside the chest to a control unit located on the outside of the body.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The control unit is a small computer that controls the functions of the pump and alerts the patient if there are any problems.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: The pump and control unit receive power through a cord connected to a battery pack.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: Finally, the control unit and battery pack are held in a lightweight patient pack.

title: ANH14141 Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD)
text_en: An LVAD may be a temporary treatment until a heart transplant can be performed, or if the patient isn't able to have a heart transplant, an LVAD may be used for destination therapy, which is long-term treatment.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: You or someone you know may have been diagnosed with high triglycerides. This video will show you what they are and how their levels are measured. High triglycerides can have harmful effects on your body.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: Triglycerides are a type of fat or lipid found in your blood. Your body uses triglycerides for energy.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: One place triglycerides come from is your liver. Another is from the food you eat. Foods that may raise your triglycerides include fatty foods such as hot dogs and tropical oils, refined carbohydrates such as french fries, foods high in simple sugars such as donuts, and alcohol.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: So how do triglycerides get to the cells that need them? Glycerides in food are digested and eventually reach your liver. In the liver, both the triglycerides from your food and those made in your liver are packaged together with proteins. Each resulting molecule is called a lipoprotein.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: The lipoproteins travel throughout the body in your bloodstream. They deliver triglycerides to your cells which use them for energy. Or if you have more than you need, fat cells store the triglycerides as body fat.

title: ANH15144 Understanding Triglycerides
text_en: If you are 20 years of age or older, the National Institutes of Health recommends you have a blood test every 5 years. The test is called a fasting lipoprotein profile. During this test, a blood sample will be taken from your arm after you have not eaten for 9-12 hours.
